steps.title: The Final Week
steps.body:
You've done something remarkable.

Week 1 — You had the courage to look honestly at your marriage.
Week 2 — You learned the skills to repair what's broken.
Week 3 — You rebuilt connection, warmth, and friendship.

And now: Week 4 — Mission.

This week isn't about fixing or patching. It's about building. Designing. Choosing.

A marriage without a mission drifts. A marriage with a mission has direction, momentum, and meaning.

What are you building together? Not just surviving — what are you creating?

This week answers that question. Not with vague visions — with something real you can build toward.

A good marriage survives the storms.
A great marriage knows where it's sailing.

Mission Week is about picking a destination — and pointing in the same direction.

questions.label:
If someone asked you, 'What's the mission of your marriage?' — what would you say?

Not what you DO together. What you're BUILDING together.

Are you building a safe harbor from the world? A launching pad for each other's growth? A legacy for your family? A partnership that makes the world better?

You might not have a clear answer. That's okay. Today is about finding one.

steps.title: The Four Dimensions of a Marriage Mission
steps.body:
A strong marriage mission usually touches four dimensions:

1. Partnership — How you show up for each other. The quality of your daily life together.
2. Growth — How you help each other become the people you want to be.
3. Legacy — What you're creating that outlasts you. Family, community, impact.
4. Adventure — How you keep life interesting, curious, and alive together.

These aren't categories to check off. They're areas to explore.

Where are you strong? Where have you drifted? Where do you want to focus your energy in this next season of life?

Not every dimension needs equal attention in every season. But they all deserve a conversation.

questions.label:
Look at the four dimensions: Partnership, Growth, Legacy, Adventure.

Rank them in order of importance to you right now.

Now guess how your partner would rank them.

Where do you align? Where might there be tension?

This isn't about being right — it's about understanding what each of you is pointing toward.

steps.question: A couple needs to agree on every detail of their shared vision for their marriage to work.
feedback: Correct. You don't need total alignment — you need mutual understanding and respect for each other's priorities. The goal is a shared conversation, not a perfect match on every detail.
Not quite. Total agreement isn't the goal. What matters is that you understand each other's priorities and can find overlap. Differences can be complementary.

steps.title: Two Different Missions
steps.body:
Partner A: 'Our mission is to create a stable, peaceful home where our kids feel safe and we can grow old together.'

Partner B: 'Our mission is to keep pushing each other to grow, take risks, and have adventures — even when it's uncomfortable.'

Neither is wrong. They're different. And if A and B never talk about this, A will feel like B is destabilizing the home, while B will feel like A is holding them back.

The answer isn't for one to convert the other. The answer is to talk about it until you find a mission that honors both.

'A stable home that's also a launching pad for each other's growth.' — that's a mission both can point toward.

steps.title: Start the Mission Conversation
steps.prompt:
Tonight, start a conversation with your partner:

'What do you want our marriage to be about? Not just day-to-day — but what are we really building together?'

Don't try to answer it tonight. Just start the conversation. Let it breathe. Come back to it.

Write what you hope to learn from this conversation:
steps.body:
Tonight, start a conversation with your partner:

'What do you want our marriage to be about? Not just day-to-day — but what are we really building together?'

Don't try to answer it tonight. Just start the conversation. Let it breathe. Come back to it.

Write what you hope to learn from this conversation:

steps.question: A marriage mission is most useful when it:
options: Perfectly describes every aspect of your relationship
Provides direction and helps you make decisions together
Sounds impressive when you tell other people
Is set once and never revisited
feedback: A mission doesn't need to cover everything. It's a compass, not a map.
Yes. A mission's value is in giving you a 'north star' — something to orient toward when you're making decisions about time, money, and priorities.
A mission isn't for other people. It's for you.
Missions evolve. Revisit yours every season of life.

steps.title: Tomorrow: Your Shared Vision
steps.body: Today you started thinking about your marriage mission. Tomorrow, we get specific — creating a shared vision for where you're going together. Not a five-year plan with bullet points. A picture of what you're building.

steps.title: Day Complete
steps.body: Great work today! You're building something real.
